metadata.notes: Wingspan standard overhaul: removed legacy sections format, added rules_citations with key rulings, validated content against official rulebook.

subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Global Components
Let's go through everything in the box. First, the big stuff.

You'll find 1 large game board — it shows a hex-grid map of Eastern Europa with territories, plus three tracks along the edges: the Triumph Track (where stars go), the Popularity Track, and the Power Track.

There's also 1 quick-reference guide that summarizes all the actions — keep it handy.

You'll see 5 player mats. These are double-sided boards labeled Industrial, Engineering, Patriotic, Mechanical, and Agricultural. Each one arranges the same 4 action columns in a different layout, so every player's economy feels different.

Now for the card decks: 42 combat cards (numbered 2 through 5 — these are your secret weapon in fights), 23 objective cards (private goals worth a star each), 28 encounter cards (story events with 3 choices), and 12 Factory cards (powerful bonus actions you can pick up mid-game).

For resources, you'll find 80 resource tokens — that's 20 each of food (orange crates), metal (silver ingots), wood (brown logs), and oil (black barrels). There are also 2 cardboard power dials for secret combat bidding, 80 cardboard coins in various denominations, 5 Riverwalk reference cards, 12 multiplier tokens for scoring, 12 encounter tokens (round discs placed on the map), 5 quick-start cards for new players, and 6 structure bonus tiles (you'll use 1 per game).

#### Per-Faction Components (x5 factions)
Each faction — Rusviet (red), Crimean (yellow), Nordic (blue), Polania (white), and Saxony (black) — gets its own set of pieces.

You'll find 1 faction mat showing your unique faction ability and 4 mech ability slots. There's 1 beautifully detailed character miniature, 4 mech miniatures, and 8 small wooden workers. For tracking, each faction gets 6 star tokens (wooden stars), 4 structure tokens, 4 recruit tokens (enlist cylinders), 6 technology cubes, and 1 each of an action token, a popularity token, and a power token.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
#### Setting Up the Board
Place the game board in the center of the table so everyone can reach it. Make sure the Triumph Track, Popularity Track, and Power Track along the edges are all visible.

Now find the 11 encounter tokens — they're round discs with a question mark symbol. Place 1 encounter token on each territory that has an encounter symbol printed on it. You should place exactly 11 tokens.

Create a supply of resource tokens and coins near the board where everyone can reach them.

Shuffle the combat cards, encounter cards, and objective cards into 3 separate face-down decks and place them near the board.

Randomly choose 1 of the 6 structure bonus tiles — place it face-up at the bottom of the Popularity Track. Return the other 5 to the box.

Shuffle the Factory cards and deal them face-down near the Factory hex in the center of the board. You need one more card than the number of players — so for 4 players, set out 5 Factory cards. Return any extras to the box.

#### Faction and Mat Assignment
Randomly deal 1 faction mat and 1 player mat to each player. The combination of faction mat plus player mat creates your unique strategic puzzle — no two players will have the same economy.

Return any unused mats and extra Factory cards to the box.

At this point, each player should have 1 faction mat and 1 player mat in front of them, and the board should have encounter tokens placed, decks shuffled, and a structure bonus tile visible.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Faction Mat Setup
Look at your faction mat — it shows your faction's starting values in the top corners.

Find your power token (a small wooden disc in your faction color) and place it on the Power Track at the starting value printed on your faction mat.

Draw the number of combat cards shown on your faction mat from the combat card deck. Keep these hidden — opponents can see how many you have, but not their values.

Take the number of coins shown on your faction mat from the supply.

#### Player Mat Setup
Now look at your player mat — it shows additional starting values.

Place your popularity token on the Popularity Track at the starting value printed on your player mat.

Draw the number of objective cards shown on your player mat. These are secret — don't show them to anyone.

Now set up your player mat's moving parts. Find the 6 green technology cubes — place them on the top-row action spaces that have small black square markers. These cubes will move to the bottom row when you Upgrade.

Place your 4 structure tokens on the 4 structure boxes on the mat. Place your 4 recruit tokens on the 4 recruit spaces. Place your 6 reserve workers on the rectangular spaces in the Produce action area.

Set your action token beside your player mat — you'll use it to mark which column you chose each turn.

#### Board Placement
Place your character miniature on your faction's home base territory on the board.

Place 2 workers on the 2 territories adjacent to your home base. These are your starting production workers.

Keep your 4 mechs on the mech spaces of your faction mat — they'll deploy to the board as you play.

#### Starting the Game
The player with the lowest-numbered player mat goes first. Play proceeds clockwise.

A few things to know about information: track positions, units on the board, and the count of each opponent's combat cards are all public. Combat card values and objective card content are hidden.

At this point, you should have your character on your home base, 2 workers on adjacent territories, your tracks set to starting values, tech cubes and structures on your player mat, and your hand of combat cards and objectives ready to go.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### What a Turn Looks Like
On your turn, you'll do the following:

**Step 1: Choose an action column.** Look at your player mat — it has 4 columns, each pairing a top-row action with a bottom-row action. Pick one column and place your action token on it.

**Step 2: The repeat restriction.** Here's the key rule — you may NOT choose the same column you used on your previous turn. You must move your action token to a different column each turn. This is what creates the rhythm of the game.

**Step 3: Resolve the top-row action.** Perform the top action of your chosen column: Move, Produce, Trade, or Bolster. This is optional — you can skip it if you want.

**Step 4: Resolve the bottom-row action.** Now you may perform the paired bottom action: Upgrade, Deploy, Build, or Enlist. You'll need to pay the resource cost shown. This is also optional.

**Step 5: Star check.** If you just completed an achievement (like deploying all 4 mechs, or winning combat), immediately place a star token on the Triumph Track.

**Step 6: End your turn.** Pass play clockwise.

#### How the Game Ends
The game ends immediately — mid-turn if necessary — when any player places their 6th star on the Triumph Track. A common mistake: thinking you finish the round. You don't. The game stops the instant that 6th star lands.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Top-Row Actions
These are the actions on the top half of each column on your player mat.

**Move** — Move up to 2 of your units (characters, mechs, or workers), each moving 1 territory. Your units can carry resources along with them when they move — just pick up the resource tokens and bring them to the new territory.

**Produce** — Choose up to 2 territories where you have workers. Each worker on those territories produces 1 resource matching the terrain type: Farms produce food, Forests produce wood, Mountains produce metal, Tundra produces oil, and Villages produce a new worker (taken from your player mat's reserve).

**Trade** — Pay 1 coin to gain 2 resources of your choice from the supply and place them on any territory you control. Alternatively, pay 1 coin to gain 1 popularity on the Popularity Track.

**Bolster** — Gain power on the Power Track and/or draw combat cards, depending on what your player mat shows for this action.

#### Bottom-Row Actions
These are the more powerful actions on the bottom half of each column. Each one has a resource cost.

**Upgrade (costs oil)** — Move 1 technology cube from a top-row action space down to a bottom-row action space. This does two things at once: it improves the top-row action's output AND reduces the bottom-row action's cost. Upgrades are one of the most efficient things you can do early.

**Deploy (costs metal)** — Take 1 mech from your faction mat and place it on any territory where you have at least 1 worker. Deploying a mech unlocks a faction-specific mech ability printed underneath it — flip that ability face-up so everyone can see it.

**Build (costs wood)** — Take 1 structure from your player mat and place it on any territory where you have at least 1 worker. Structures give permanent effects and help you control territory.

**Enlist (costs food)** — Remove 1 recruit token from your player mat. You immediately gain a one-time bonus shown on the space, and you permanently gain an ongoing bonus that triggers whenever you OR the player to your left or right takes that same bottom-row action.

#### Key Rules to Remember
You choose ONE column per turn — the top and bottom action must come from the same column. Both actions are optional — you can skip either or both. Resources are spent from territories you control, and you can combine resources across multiple territories to pay a cost. A common mistake: workers are NOT resources and cannot be spent as costs.

subtopics.title: Combat
subtopics.content:
#### When Combat Happens
Combat triggers when your character or mech moves into a territory occupied by an opponent's character or mech. Important: workers alone don't trigger combat — only characters and mechs are combat units.

#### How to Resolve Combat
Here's what happens, step by step:

**1. Set your power dial.** Both you and your opponent simultaneously and secretly choose how much power to spend — anywhere from 0 up to your current power level. Turn the dial to your chosen number without showing it.

**2. Add combat cards.** Each player may add up to 1 combat card per character or mech they have in the fight. So if you attacked with your character and 1 mech, you could play up to 2 combat cards.

**3. Reveal!** Both players flip their dials. Add your dial number plus any combat card values. The highest total wins. If there's a tie, the attacker wins.

**4. Spend the power.** Both players — winner AND loser — lose the power they bid. It's gone from the Power Track regardless of the outcome.

#### After Combat
The **winner** stays on the territory and places a star on the Triumph Track for a combat victory. You can earn at most 2 combat stars this way — except Saxony, who has no limit.

The **loser's** units all retreat back to their home base. Any resources on that territory stay behind — the winner now controls them.

Here's the important part about workers: if the loser had workers on the territory, those workers also retreat home. The loser draws 1 combat card for each retreating worker (small consolation). But the winner loses 1 popularity for each opponent worker they displaced. A common mistake: forgetting the popularity cost. Attacking a territory with 3 enemy workers costs you 3 popularity — that's nearly a full scoring tier!

#### Combat Tips
Combat card count is public information — use it to estimate your opponent's strength before committing. You can never attack someone on their home base.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Encounters
When your character reaches a territory with an encounter token on it, pick up the token and draw an encounter card. Each card tells a short story and offers you 3 choices — pick the one that best suits your situation. Only your character can resolve encounters, not mechs or workers. Encounters are almost always worth a detour — the rewards are generous.

#### The Factory
The Factory is the large hex in the center of the board. The first time your character reaches it, you get to look at the face-up Factory cards and choose 1. This card becomes a permanent 5th action column on your player mat — a powerful bonus action you can use for the rest of the game.

At end-game scoring, the Factory counts as 3 territories instead of 1 for whoever controls it. Each player can only claim 1 Factory card per game.

#### Structures
There are 4 types of structures, each with a permanent effect:

**Mine** — Your character and mechs can now use tunnel movement, traveling between any territories marked with tunnel symbols on the board.

**Mill** — The territory where you built the Mill counts as an extra Produce territory for free — you don't need a worker there to produce.

**Armory** — Whenever you take the Bolster action, gain +1 power beyond what your player mat shows.

**Monument** — Whenever you take the Bolster action, gain +1 popularity beyond what your player mat shows.

Structures stay on the board permanently for the entire game. You control any territory with your structure on it, even if you have no units there — unless an opponent moves their units onto that territory.

#### Stars (Achievements)
You can earn stars by completing these achievements. Each star placed on the Triumph Track brings you closer to triggering the end:
- Complete all 6 upgrades
- Deploy all 4 mechs
- Build all 4 structures
- Enlist all 4 recruits
- Place all 8 workers
- Complete 1 objective card
- Win combat (maximum 2 combat stars per player — Saxony has no limit)
- Reach 18 popularity
- Reach 16 power

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### When Does the Game End?
The game ends immediately when any player places their 6th star on the Triumph Track. No finishing the round — it stops right then.

#### How to Calculate Your Score
Your final score is measured in coins. Add up everything:

**Coins on hand** — All the coins you've accumulated during the game.

**Stars, territories, and resources** — Here's where it gets interesting. Your popularity level determines a multiplier for each of these three categories:

| What You're Scoring | Tier 1 (0-6 popularity) | Tier 2 (7-12 popularity) | Tier 3 (13-18 popularity) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Each star you placed | 3 coins | 4 coins | 5 coins |
| Each territory you control | 2 coins | 3 coins | 4 coins |
| Every 2 resources you control | 1 coin | 2 coins | 3 coins |

This is why popularity matters so much — the difference between Tier 1 and Tier 3 can be 30+ coins.

#### Counting Territories
Count every territory where you have at least 1 unit (worker, character, or mech) or an uncontested structure (no opponent units present). The Factory counts as 3 territories for whoever controls it. A common mistake: your home base does NOT count as a territory for scoring.

#### Structure Bonus
Remember that randomly selected structure bonus tile from setup? Score it now based on where you placed your structures on the board.

#### Tiebreakers
If scores are tied: 1. Most coins on hand, then 2. Most territories, then 3. Most resources, then 4. Most stars.

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Opening Priorities

**Build a repeatable 2-3 turn loop.** This is the single most important thing in Scythe. Because you can't pick the same column twice in a row, you need to plan which columns you'll alternate between. Your first 3 turns should flow naturally without forcing a dead turn. For example: Produce on turn 1, then Move on turn 2, then back to Produce.

**Upgrade early.** One early Upgrade is incredibly efficient — it improves a top-row action's output AND lowers a bottom-row cost at the same time. If you can Upgrade on turn 1 or 2, do it.

**Expand workers onto productive terrain.** More workers means more resources when you Produce, which means faster bottom-row completion, which means more stars. Push workers out of your starting area toward farms, forests, mountains, and tundra.

**Protect your resources.** Resources sit on territories, not in your hand. If you leave a pile of 5 metal on an exposed frontier, an opponent can walk in, push you out, and claim it all. Keep valuable resource stacks on territories you can defend.

**Track popularity from turn 1.** Popularity multiplies ALL three end-game scoring categories — stars, territories, and resources. Even a great board position with Tier 1 popularity will lose to a decent board at Tier 3.

**Use encounters for tempo.** Route your character toward encounter tokens early. Each encounter gives you a free bonus from 3 options — that's a resource swing you didn't have to spend an action on.

#### Common Mistakes
- Picking a strong action now without planning what you'll do next turn (remember, you can't repeat the same column)
- Chasing extra combats after you've already earned your 2 combat stars — those fights cost power and popularity for zero additional stars
- Leaving large resource stacks on undefended frontier territories where opponents can swoop in
- Forgetting about your private objective cards until it's too late to pivot toward completing them
- Neglecting popularity — a strong board position at Tier 1 popularity scores far less than a modest board at Tier 3
- Rushing to place your 6th star while behind in territory, resources, and popularity conversion — triggering the end doesn't help if your score is lower than everyone else's

subtopics.title: First-Game Walkthrough
subtopics.content:
#### Your First Game of Scythe

**The Setup Decision**
You've been dealt a faction mat (your unique abilities and starting position on the map) and a player mat (your action economy — how the 4 columns are arranged and what they cost). The combination creates your unique strategic puzzle.

Place your character on your home base hex. Place 2 workers on the two territories next to your home base. Set your power, popularity, combat cards, and coins to the starting values shown on your mats.

**Understanding Your Player Mat**
Take a moment to study your player mat before the game starts. It has 4 columns, and each column pairs a top-row action with a bottom-row action. On your turn, you'll pick ONE column — you can do the top action, the bottom action, or both. The catch: you CANNOT pick the same column you used on your previous turn.

See the green technology cubes sitting on some top-row actions? When you take the Upgrade action, you'll move these cubes down to bottom-row actions. Each cube moved improves the top AND reduces the bottom cost. That's why Upgrading early is so good.

**Turns 1-3: Getting Your Engine Started**

*Turn 1*: Find a column with Produce on top. Place your action token there and produce resources with your 2 starting workers — each worker generates 1 resource matching its terrain. If the bottom-row action in that column is affordable, take it too.

*Turn 2*: Switch to a DIFFERENT column. Good early options are Trade (pay 1 coin for 2 resources you need) or Bolster (gain power and combat cards). If you can afford the bottom-row action — especially Upgrade — take it.

*Turn 3*: Another different column. Consider Moving your character toward an encounter token. Encounters give you free resources and bonuses just for showing up.

**The Rhythm (Turns 4-10)**
By now you should feel the 2-turn loop forming: Produce resources one turn, spend them on a bottom-row action the next. Every bottom-row action you complete (Upgrade, Deploy, Build, Enlist) gets you closer to a star. Deploy your mechs for better mobility and combat readiness. Build structures for permanent bonuses on key territories.

**Mid-Game: Combat and Territory**
Once you have mechs on the board, you can start contesting territories. Before picking a fight, check your power level against your opponent's, and count their combat cards. Only fight when you have a clear advantage or when you need a combat star. Remember — each displaced enemy worker costs you 1 popularity.

**End Game: The Star Race**
Keep your eye on everyone's star count. When someone places their 6th star, the game ends IMMEDIATELY — no finishing the round. Make sure your popularity is at Tier 2 or higher (7+), you're controlling plenty of territories, and your resources are stacked up for the final scoring multiplier.

**First Game Target**: Focus on building a smooth 2-turn loop. Aim for 3-4 stars. Get your popularity to Tier 2 (7+). A score of 40-60 coins is solid for a first game.

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Faction Strategy Guide

**Rusviet Union (Red) — Top Tier**
Rusviet's unique ability breaks the core game restriction: you CAN repeat the same player mat column two turns in a row. This makes Rusviet the most efficient faction in the game — find your strongest column and spam it relentlessly. Your engine starts faster than everyone else's.

Key mechs to unlock: Township lets your character and mechs teleport to any village or the Factory. People's Army makes your workers add +1 power each in combat.

**Crimean Khanate (Yellow) — High Tier**
Your unique ability lets you spend combat cards as if they were any resource type. This means you're never resource-starved as long as you Bolster for combat cards. Opening plan: Bolster heavily in your first few turns, then use combat cards to rush through bottom-row actions.

Key mech: Scout lets you steal 1 opponent combat card before combat even begins.

**Nordic Kingdoms (Blue) — Mid Tier**
Your workers can swim across rivers without needing the Riverwalk ability. This gives you the fastest early map expansion — spread workers onto diverse terrain types before anyone else can cross rivers. Opening plan: push workers across rivers immediately to claim productive territories.

Key mechs: Seaworthy lets you move across lakes. Artillery subtracts 2 from your opponent's combat power.

**Polania Republic (White) — Mid-High Tier**
Your unique ability lets you choose 2 encounter options instead of just 1. This makes encounters incredibly powerful — rush your character toward encounter tokens as fast as possible.

Key mech: Camaraderie means you suffer no popularity loss when you displace opponent workers in combat. This removes the biggest downside of aggressive play.

**Saxony Empire (Black) — Variable Tier**
Your unique ability removes the limit on combat and objective stars. While every other faction caps at 2 combat stars, you can earn a star for every single combat victory. Opening plan: expand and fight early. Every combat win is a star with no cap.

The danger: if opponents avoid fighting you, your aggression yields fewer stars than a more balanced approach.

#### Player Mat Combos
- **Rusviet + Industrial**: Widely considered the strongest combination in the game. The fast engine plus the ability to repeat columns is dominant.
- **Crimea + Patriotic**: Resource flexibility from combat cards combined with strong enlist bonuses creates a dominant mid-game.
- **Saxony + Mechanical**: Cheap mech deployment gets you into combat fast, and your unlimited combat stars reward the aggression.

#### Combat Strategy
**Information advantage**: Your opponent's combat card count is public, and their power level is on the track for all to see. Calculate before committing to a fight.

**Spend the minimum to win**: Overspending power is wasteful — bid just enough to beat what you think your opponent will commit.

**The zero bluff**: Sometimes bidding 0 power is the right call. If your opponent overspends to beat you, they waste power. You lose nothing, retreat home, and your retreating workers even give you free combat cards.

**Watch the popularity cost**: Each displaced opponent worker costs you 1 popularity. Attacking a territory with 3 enemy workers means losing 3 popularity — nearly a full scoring tier. Make sure the territory is worth it.

#### Star Rush vs Economic Play
**Star Rush**: Place 6 stars as fast as possible to end the game while opponents are still building. Best for Saxony (unlimited combat stars) and Rusviet (repeat columns for speed).

**Economic Play**: Focus on reaching Tier 3 popularity, holding many territories, and stockpiling resources. Let the scoring multipliers win the math. Best for Nordic (early expansion) and Polania (encounter bonuses).

**Balanced (recommended for most games)**: Aim for 4-5 stars at a good pace, Tier 2+ popularity, and 5+ territories.

#### Key Decision Points
1. **Turns 1-3: Loop design** — The 2-3 turn repeating pattern you establish here shapes your entire game. Get it wrong and you'll feel stuck all game.
2. **Turns 5-8: Factory timing** — Is it worth detouring your character to the Factory for a 5th action column? Or should you stay local and keep producing? The Factory also counts as 3 territories for scoring.
3. **Mid-game: First combat** — Your first fight is the most impactful. Time it when you have a clear power advantage.
4. **Late game: End timing** — Before placing your 6th star, do the math. Would 1-2 more turns to improve your popularity or territory control score you more coins than ending now?

#### Player Count Adjustments
- **2 Players**: The map is huge with just 2 — combat is easily avoidable. Focus on engine efficiency and completing objectives.
- **3 Players**: Well balanced. Each faction has room to breathe, but territories start mattering for scoring.
- **4-5 Players**: The map gets tight and combat becomes frequent. Popularity management is critical because displacement penalties add up fast. Territory control drives the final scores.

key_rulings.text: If the attacking player has the same total power as the defending player (including combat card values), the attacker wins.
You may not place your action token on the same section of your player mat as the previous turn. You must move your action token to a different section each turn.
Workers cannot move into a territory controlled by an opponent's workers, character, or mechs. Only characters and mechs can initiate combat by moving into an opponent-controlled territory.
At the end of the game, the Factory is worth a total of 3 territories (instead of 1) to the player who controls it.

key_rulings.topic: Home base is not a territory
key_rulings.text: Your home base is not a territory. It does not count for end-game scoring. You cannot move units into an opponent's home base.
If you win combat and your opponent has workers on the territory, each of those workers retreats to the opponent's home base, and you lose 1 popularity for each worker displaced.
Each player may place a maximum of 2 stars from winning combat. The Saxony Empire faction is the exception — they have no limit on the number of combat or objective stars they may place.
Resources are not owned by players. They are on specific territories. You control resources on territories where you have at least one unit (worker, character, or mech) or an uncontested structure.
If you produce on a territory with 1-2 workers, there is no additional cost. Producing on territories with 3-4 total workers costs 1 power. 5-6 workers costs 2 power. 7-8 workers costs 3 power.

A structure on a territory means you control that territory even without any units present — unless an opponent moves units onto that territory. Structures remain permanently on the board even if an opponent takes the territory.
